channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: San Andreas fault reaches highest stress level in 1,000 years, study say - WYFF News 4
item.description:
1. San Andreas fault reaches highest stress level in 1,000 years, study say  WYFF News 4
  2. One of America's most dangerous fault lines reaches highest stress levels in 1,000 years, study finds  FOX Weather
  3. California’s tectonic systems at highest levels of stress in 1,000 years – study  The Guardian
  4. Chance of mega earthquake hitting California now at a 'historic high', experts warn  BBC Science Focus Magazine
  5. 'The system is critically stressed': San Andreas and San Jacinto faults scarily close to major earthquake, study finds  Live Science

item.title: The Milky Way’s huge black hole was missing a key feature. Scientists say they finally detected it - CNN
item.description:

  3. Black hole winds racing at 30% of light speed would hit Earth like a Category 79 hurricane  The Times of India

  4. Bose-Einstein Condensate Evolves Across 44 Cycles Of Recollapse  Quantum Zeitgeist

item.title: Beneath the Antarctic ice, a lake the size of Lake Ontario has been sealed off from sunlight for roughly 15 million years, and when Russian drillers finally reached Lake Vostok in 2012 they recovered water teeming with traces of life that had evolved in total dark - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Beneath the Antarctic ice, a lake the size of Lake Ontario has been sealed off from sunlight for roughly 15 million years, and when Russian drillers finally reached Lake Vostok in 2012 they recovered water teeming with traces of life that had evolved in total dark  Space Daily
  2. What AI Found Beneath Antarctica Shocked Scientists  vocal.media

item.title: When John McFall lost his right leg at 19, no one could have known the former Paralympic sprinter might one day carry the first amputee body into orbit, where two weeks in microgravity could test what six decades of spaceflight medicine never measured - Space Daily
item.description: When John McFall lost his right leg at 19, no one could have known the former Paralympic sprinter might one day carry the first amputee body into orbit, where two weeks in microgravity could test what six decades of spaceflight medicine never measured  Space Daily

item.description:
1. Deep learning helps discover hundreds of Antarctic earthquakes coming from an unlikely location  Phys.org

  3. Scientists Detect Hundreds of Earthquakes Deep Beneath Antarctica  Yahoo
  4. 500 hidden earthquakes beneath Antarctica, and the strangest ones are shaking the middle of a tectonic pla  The Economic Times
  5. Forget the image of a frozen and still continent: scientists have just revealed hundreds of earthquakes beneath East Antarctica, in an area where no one expected to find this phenomenon.  CPG Click Petróleo e Gás

  3. First working nuclear clock heralds a new era in timekeeping  New Scientist
  4. Clocks made from an atomic nucleus just ticked on for the first time  Science News
  5. Dark Matter And Fundamental Physics Are Exciting Applications for First-of-Its-Kind Nuclear Clock.  IFLScience

item.title: When the Soviet Venera 13 probe touched down on Venus in March 1982, it survived for 127 minutes in a 465-degree atmosphere that crushed it with the pressure of nearly a kilometre of ocean water, and in that time it scraped a soil sample, analysed it, and tr - Space Daily
item.description: When the Soviet Venera 13 probe touched down on Venus in March 1982, it survived for 127 minutes in a 465-degree atmosphere that crushed it with the pressure of nearly a kilometre of ocean water, and in that time it scraped a soil sample, analysed it, and tr  Space Daily

item.title: Mars can produce dust storms so vast they swallow the planet. In 2018, one of them turned day into darkness for NASA’s solar-powered Opportunity rover, cutting off the sunlight that had sustained it through more than 14 years on Mars. - Space Daily
item.description: Mars can produce dust storms so vast they swallow the planet. In 2018, one of them turned day into darkness for NASA’s solar-powered Opportunity rover, cutting off the sunlight that had sustained it through more than 14 years on Mars.  Space Daily
